passages.title: The Rise of Telecommuting
passages.source_html:
The Rise of Telecommuting


Telecommuting, or 'remote work', is an arrangement in which employees do not commute to a place of work


A


The most complete definition of a 'telecommuter' is someone employed full time at a private, nonprofit or government organization, who works at least half the time at home. By one estimate, telecommuting rose 79 percent between 2005 and 2012 and by 2014 made up 2.6 percent of the work force in the United States, or 3.2 million workers, according to the American Community Survey.


However, the scope of telecommuting has at times been expanded to include the self-employed; those whose work has to be done outside an office, such as taxi drivers, plumbers and construction workers; companies where everyone works remotely, so there is no centralized office; and those who work at home one day or less a week. If all of those workers are included, the number of Americans who work remotely can reach as high as 30 percent.


Jennifer Glass, a professor of sociology at the University of Texas in the United States, has studied teleworking for two decades, and says much of what managers and professionals call telecommuting occurs after a 40-hour week spent in the office. People check email, return calls and write reports from home, but in the evenings and weekends. This is not strictly telecommuting as the general population would understand it.


B


There is a widespread perception that telecommuting is usually done by someone in their 20s, or a mother with small children. In fact, according to numbers from the Census Bureau's annual American Community Survey, the typical telecommuter is a 49-year-old college graduate – man or woman – who belongs to a company with more than 100 employees. The phenomenon appears to be growing: the annual survey last year by the Society for Human Resource Management found a greater increase in the number of companies planning to offer telecommuting than those offering any other new benefit. And it seems that winter conditions might push the trend even faster. Federal employees in Washington, who worked from home recently during four official snow days, saved the government an estimated $32 million, according to Kate Lister, president of Global Workplace Analytics.


C


Kipp Jarecke-Cheng fits the typical teleworker profile. He is 44, and for the last year has been director of global public relations and communications for a design and technology consulting company based in Montreal in Canada. At his old job, he commuted about 45 minutes to Manhattan from his home in New Jersey, but he chose to telecommute to be closer to his wife and children. Jarecke-Cheng found that it took some time to get used to working at home. Like many teleworkers, he found that communicating with his colleagues and subordinates was more difficult, at least initially. 'Probably one of the biggest transitions was that in a physical office, you can stroll by and ask questions,' says Jarecke-Cheng. 'Here I have to accumulate a list of questions.' But it helped tremendously, he said, that after he was hired, his employer sent him to its national and international offices to see people face-to-face. 'I'm not just a name on an email,' he explains.


D


David Haddad of New York City began working remotely as chief executive of a nonprofit start-up in 2011. His start-up is made up solely of telecommuters, with no centralized location. Haddad likes the flexibility, but there are downsides. 'It's difficult to keep tabs of what everyone is doing, as well as keep myself motivated to constantly report on my goals,' he said. 


While technology can't replace the human connection, Haddad says it makes communicating easier. Video conferencing is used, as are communications apps which provide virtual places for people to drop in and 'visit'. For some people, virtual connections are enough, while for others nothing takes the place of being able to chat with a colleague over an office cubicle divider.


E


Nicholas Bloom, a professor of economics at Stanford University in the US, teamed up with China's largest travel agency to test some ideas about telecommuting. Over nine months, about 250 workers volunteered for the experiment; half were randomly chosen to work at home and half in the office. At the end of the experiment, employers found that the home-based employees worked more than office workers – 9.5 percent longer – and were 13 percent more productive. Also, by reducing office space, the company saved what would amount to US$2,000 per employee annually. Bloom reports that the home-based workers also were judged to be happier, as quitting rates were cut in half. However, those working at home were also promoted at half the rate of their colleagues working in the office. 'It may be a case of "out of sight, out of mind,"' Professor Bloom said. 'Or it might be that you're not drinking in the bar with your boss. Or it could be you' re not managing your employees as well if you' re not around them.'


F


Interestingly, at the end of the experiment, 50 percent of those who worked at home asked to come back to the office. They said they were lonely and didn't like being passed over for promotion. It does seem that most people like to have some combination of home and office work, according to Lister, 'that's the sweet spot'.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14 - 19


Look at the six sections (A - F). Which section contains the following information?

Choose the correct letter (A - F). You may choose any letter more than once.

Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
14

14 an example of a company where all of the employees work remotely
✅ 正确答案：D

定位词: all employees work remotely


定位原文: D段，His start-up is made up solely of telecommuters, with no centralized location.


详细解析：D段指出，David Haddad 的公司完全由远程工作的员工组成，且没有集中办公地点。这里的solely of telecommuters 意味着公司所有员工都远程办公，且no centralized location 说明没有集中式的办公室环境。这与题干中一个所有员工远程办公的公司的描述完全对应。


同义替换：all employees work remotely = solely of telecommuters
15

15 an explanation of why some workers chose to stop telecommuting once they had tried it
✅ 正确答案：F

定位词: stop telecommuting


定位原文: F段，Interestingly, at the end of the experiment, 50 percent of those who worked at home asked to come back to the office. They said they were lonely and didn't like being passed over for promotion. 


详细解析：F段指出，在实验结束后，有一半的远程工作者选择回到办公室，原因是孤独感（lonely）和错失晋升机会（being passed over for promotion）。这些原因清楚地解释了为什么一些员工在尝试远程办公后选择停止。


同义替换：stop telecommuting = come back to the office
16

16 a discussion of the kinds of working arrangements that qualify as telecommuting
✅ 正确答案：A

定位词: kinds of working arrangements 和 qualify as telecommuting


定位原文: A段，However, the scope of telecommuting has at times been expanded to include the self-employed; those whose work has to be done outside an office, such as taxi drivers, plumbers and construction workers; companies where everyone works remotely, so there is no centralized office; and those who work at home one day or less a week.


详细解析：A段指出了多种符合远程办公定义的工作形式，包括自雇人士（self-employed）、户外工作者（e.g., taxi drivers, plumbers）、完全远程办公的公司，以及部分时间远程工作者。这是对题干远程办公形式的讨论的直接回答。


同义替换：kinds of = scope of
17

17 a comparison between telecommuters and other workers in terms of efficiency
✅ 正确答案：E

定位词: comparison， efficiency


定位原文: E段，At the end of the experiment, employers found that the home-based employees worked more than office workers – 9.5 percent longer – and were 13 percent more productive.


详细解析：E段指出，实验结果显示远程员工比办公室员工工作时间更长（9.5% longer）且效率更高（13% more productive）。这是对远程员工与办公室员工在效率上的对比。


同义替换：efficiency = productive
18

18 an example of a person who is telecommuting for family reasons
✅ 正确答案：C

定位词: telecommuting for family reasons


定位原文: C段，At his old job, he commuted about 45 minutes to Manhattan from his home in New Jersey, but he chose to telecommute to be closer to his wife and children. 


详细解析：C段指出，Kipp Jarecke-Cheng 选择远程办公是为了与家人（妻子和孩子）更亲近，这是典型的因家庭原因选择远程办公的例子。
19

19 reasons why teleworkers may have fewer career opportunities than workers in offices
✅ 正确答案：E

定位词: fewer career opportunities


定位原文: E段，However, those working at home were also promoted at half the rate of their colleagues working in the office. 'It may be a case of "out of sight, out of mind,"' Professor Bloom said. 'Or it might be that you're not drinking in the bar with your boss. Or it could be you' re not managing your employees as well if you' re not around them.'


详细解析：E段指出，远程员工的晋升机会只有办公室员工的一半（promoted at half the rate），原因包括眼不见心不想（out of sight, out of mind）以及缺乏面对面的管理机会。


同义替换：fewer career opportunities = promoted at half the rate

Questions 20 - 23


Choose the correct person (A - D) for each statement. You may choose any correct person more than once.


List of People

A Jennifer Glass

B Kate Lister

C Kipp Jarecke-Cheng

D Nicholas Bloom

Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
20

20 Finding out information from co-workers is a less casual process for a telecommuter.
✅ 正确答案：C

定位词:Kipp Jarecke-Cheng


定位原文: C段，'Probably one of the biggest transitions was that in a physical office, you can stroll by and ask questions,' says Jarecke-Cheng.


详细解析：C段指出，Kipp Jarecke-Cheng 表示，在远程工作中，获取信息的方式变得不如在办公室随意。他需要积累问题列表，而不是像在办公室一样走过去直接提问。
21

21 Telecommuters were less likely than office workers to leave their jobs.
✅ 正确答案：D

定位词: Nicholas Bloom


定位原文: E段，Bloom reports that the home-based workers also were judged to be happier, as quitting rates were cut in half.


详细解析：E段指出，Nicholas Bloom表示，远程员工的离职率减少了一半（quitting rates were cut in half），表明他们比办公室员工更倾向于留在岗位上。


同义替换：less likely to leave their jobs = quitting rates were cut in half
22

22 Bad weather proved cost-effective for an employer. 
✅ 正确答案：B

定位词:Kate Lister


定位原文: B段，Federal employees in Washington, who worked from home recently during four official snow days, saved the government an estimated $32 million, according to Kate Lister, president of Global Workplace Analytics.


详细解析：B段指出，Kate Lister表示，恶劣天气（雪天）期间，远程办公为政府节省了约3200万美元。这表明在恶劣天气情况下，远程办公对雇主来说是具有成本效益的。
23

23 Meeting people initially in person is beneficial for someone who works from home.
✅ 正确答案：C

定位词:Kipp Jarecke-Cheng


定位原文: C段，But it helped tremendously, he said, that after he was hired, his employer sent him to its national and international offices to see people face-to-face. 


详细解析：C段指出，Kipp Jarecke-Cheng 表示，雇主安排他在开始远程工作之前与同事面对面见面，这对他的工作帮助很大。


同义替换：meeting people initially in person = see people face-to-face

Questions 24 - 26


Complete the summary. Write ONE WORD ONLY in each gap.

24

David Haddad, CEO

David Haddad leads an organisation that does not have a workplace. For Haddad, is an advantage of telecommuting. However, he finds it challenging to keep track of workers' activities and to keep informing people about his . Although it is not the same as live personal contact. Haddad finds that technology helps people interact.  

正确答案： 24 centralized  |  25 flexibility  |  26 goals
Question 24

定位词: an organisation, does not have


定位原文: D段，His start-up is made up solely of telecommuters, with no centralized location.


详细解析：D段指出，David Haddad 的初创公司完全由远程员工组成，并且明确提到no centralized location（没有集中办公地点）。


Question 25

定位词: an advantage of telecommuting


定位原文: D段，Haddad likes the flexibility, but there are downsides.


详细解析：D段指出，David Haddad 认为远程办公的主要优势是flexibility（灵活性）。这是对题干中远程办公的优点的直接回答。


同义替换：an advantage of = likes


Question 26

定位词: challenge, informing people about his


定位原文: D段，'It's difficult to keep tabs of what everyone is doing, as well as keep myself motivated to constantly report on my goals,' he said. 


详细解析：D段指出，David Haddad 认为难以跟踪员工的活动，并且不断汇报自己的目标（report on my goals）也是一项挑战。


同义替换：informing = report
